item.content:encoded:
Despite the doctor with the name of a pornstar, this was a good read.
The ending felt like a punch in the gut, but in a good way. I truly enjoyed this, and it hurt seeing the doctor and scip treated that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I feel awkward pointing this out, but…. Angela White is the name of a pornstar. lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: my comment would basically be a copy and paste of this one. kind of want a followup with the ethics committee. definitely a +1 from me as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The majority of this SCP was great and I have no issues with it. The ending however seems needlessly cruel on the Foundation's part. It feels like this SCP should have just been transfered into a new, isolated server and doesn't really seem like it needed to be decommissioned. Yes it does have a lot of potential ways to cause problems, but the Foundation already contains other anomalous A.I., such as SCP-079 and that one is actively aggressive/anti-human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My initial reaction to the ending was positive. I like that it relies on the reader's assumptions and vague implications rather than directly telegraphing what happened; it helps to spruce up the narrative make it feel more original. To a degree, I agree with many of the others that it is very abrupt, however, I think it works for the most part. I've seen similar ideas executed elsewhere and adding in more from the researcher in White's position rarely helps because we basically already know what they think and stating it outright is unnecessary.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I followed nearly the exact train of thought with this. The dialogue was so good I upvoted mid-read, and then the ending kind of stole any sort of character-based resolution from under me. I would definitely have liked to see the argument that Dr. White had with her superiors about this and its omission was disappointing. I was torn between a novote and a downvote based on that, but I think I'll settle for the novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing this sentiment, except I didn’t find the dialogue that great. The ending just felt sudden.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing sentiments from others praising the 'nom's dialogue and unique analysis of Dr Suess' story, which is where I upvoted.

I switched to a downvote, however, due to my disappointment with the ending — as it sloppily washes over what seems like a non-insignificant portion of the story.

So, I can on some level buy that White lashed out in some fashion against the DeComm, receiving a reprimand in the process, but what I feel is missing is the steps that lead to whatever action she took that merited disciplinary measures…

There is certainly a case that could be made for permitting the continued existence of 5716 - a being quite literally comprised of psychiatric assessments of contained entities has to have at least some level of appeal such that it could be argued that it would be a massive boon to the containment of humanoid entities (the fact that the Foundation in this article *has* a Psychiatric Care division would suggest that this version of the SCPF understands the need for taking care of their charges' mental health)… that then seemingly level-headed and intelligent White wouldn't try to appeal the Decomm through mundane measures prior to whatever was glossed over in the "ending" here seems a bit out-of-character.

All in all, it was an excellent story, but only 3/4's… not something that felt actually complete, and I can't support something that doesn't wholly satisfy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That ending felt like a smack in the face. I was starting to like 5716, and then you went and did that to me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of the hello world song by louie zong because both works make me sad af +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for that gut punch at the end: the "cold, not cruel" comes through pretty effectively, maybe a little too much "cruel," but all in all this tells a compelling story!

item.title: Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
+ Crit
– hide block

As ARD already mentioned, the robot dialogue here is excellent. That Cat in the Hat section is genuinely one of the funniest things I've read recently. I'm personally not a fan of the monospaced text, since it's smaller and harder to read, but it isn't intrusive to the point where I couldn't finish.

What wasn't excellent, however, was the ending. Cruelty like this is pretty unoriginal, and (while I totally get that this was sort of the intention) it cut the story short in an overly brief, unsatisfying way. While the whole narrative (researcher gets attached to anomaly, Foundation is cruel about it) isn't especially unique, the first 70% supports itself with the excellent interactions between characters. This last part doesn't, and it definitely emphasises the similarities between this and other works, many of which handled the ending better.

Overall, I'm still tentatively upvoting this, because the parts that are good are just that good.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for sad robots, and 5716 has some genuinely great dialogue in here. Their analysis of the Cat in the Hat is just delightful.

item.title: You've been acting awful tough lately...
item.content:encoded:
You're vulnerable, you're vulnerable
You are not a robot
You're lovable, so lovable
But you're just troubled

You can try to make a difference, but sometimes corporate comes out of nowhere to stomp down on it.

Thanks to Cybersqyd, anorrack, MalyceGraves, DianaBerry, and Stormbreath for taking a look at my Corpsecon entry. Thank you to k1ttybot for writing the conprocs.

Check out my author page for more cool articles!
